author: @aaronlarsen7447
text: Including the electric generator would make the Stirling design practical as an emergency or camp cook stove, heater, power station, for l.e.d. light and charging up batteries, for small tools, and electronic devices.  A steam turbine would require water, but it would be more efficient. It would be noisy as heck. A steam piston engine would require water, be more heavy, costly, more complicated still, and can involve explosions.

author: @AttilaBlade
text: Woohoo Robert, this nice demo unit running well! :) Try to insulate the water jacket from the really hot chimney, because of the heat loss between hot and cold side. I know the water boiling, so the temperature not much as 100C degrees but it could be lower. The Stirling unit actually is not so powerful, because of the low pressure inside. But if you use that speaker spider and spring combination than the pinhole will be unnecessary, and the unit will autostart if the parameters are good enough. Also I do not see any regenerator on your displacer. That could dramatically increase the power. I use some middle grade steel wool wrapped around the displacer with a thin layer only. Do not stuck in the main cylinder, it should move freely as possible, but the air should goes trough the steel wool. For the fixation I used Super bond and sodium bicarbonate powder only on the top of the displacer around the top ring. put some adhesive to where the steel wool connected to the displacer's wall and sprinkle some bicarbonate there. This combo will be hard in a second as some ceramic material. When you finnished just shake the left bicarbonate from the steel wool material. Good luck! I follow your work because I'm interesting what will happening next! :) I wish You all the best! BLADE

author: @peterclark6290
text:
Extending the horizontal tube and placing multiple Stirling bases _after the secondary air inlet_ could extract every bit of the power the burnt material could provide. 
A heat-resistant coating (e.g. ceramic) would help to contain the heat inside the housing and a level of safety.
The metal structure provides an solid foundation for a crankshaft to connect the multiple Stirling units.
An enclosed water jacket could lead to adding a simple and small steam-powered generator as well: also providing the starter unit for the Stirlings if connected to their crankshaft?
The un-engineer's minds boggles. The upcoming Winter that Europe will experience with reduced access to energy may have a solution if this went into production.

Can't wait to see where this goes 👍.

author: @Berkana
text: 3:20 The Carnot limit is not capped at 37%; it is determined by the temperature difference available to it. If you heat the hot side much hotter  with a hotter heat source or figure out some way to cool the heat sink colder, the efficiency will be higher, if you carry out proper regeneration.

replies.author: @j.christie2594
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering you have a Brillany mind I had thought of this very idea.  I grew up in country with wood stove heating, always thought that a stream engine should be part of wood stove. I didn't know about Stirling engines then. The  "oldfordboy channel" made a portable Stirling fan that sat on stove top to circulate heat off oven/stove ( I love that idea) . But No one has yet (till you) put generator to this on YT. I thought wood stove could be with steam power for both and my uncle also added "hot-water" system to his wood stove for winter use, which worked great but he didn't go with steam to move water though he went Electric, so power-outtages NO Hot water pump. Stirling could work great off reservoir with all the stored heat. I thought steam engine was a Match, Stirlings though better match,for the job II think. Again, Keep Teaching your Brillant Ideas to us. Love your Teaching and Sharing. Your channel Rocks Sir, cheers to Education👏🙌👏👏
